front: Dynasty: final imperial dynasty of China, founded by Manchu conquerors, which supplanted the Ming dynasty
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: its most celebrated rulers were the Kangxi and Qianlong Emperors
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: ended in 1912 when the last emperor, Puyi, abdicated following the Xinhai Revolution
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: Jurchen chieftain of the Aisin Gioro clan who issued the "Seven Grievances" against the Ming and won the Battle of Sarhu, organizing his people into the Eight Banners
work: Manchu Conquest and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Figure: was wounded in a defeat at the Battle of Ningyuan and died soon after
work: Manchu Conquest and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Figure: renamed the Jurchen people "Manchu" and founded the Qing dynasty proper, succeeding his father Nurhaci
work: Manchu Conquest and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Event: the Manchus sent envoys south to form this pact, which split conquests from a proposed joint invasion
work: Manchu Conquest and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Event: after the rebel Li Zicheng sacked Beijing, Ming general Wu Sangui let Manchu forces under regent Prince Dorgon through this pass, bypassing the Great Wall, to defeat Li Zicheng's army in 1644
work: Manchu Conquest and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Event: the new dynasty forced Han Chinese men to wear this hairstyle to enforce assimilation to Manchu customs, provoking a two-and-a-half-month resistance and massacre at Jiangyin
work: Manchu Conquest and the Founding of the Dynasty

front: Ruler: reigned 1661-1722, commissioned a namesake dictionary and the encyclopedia A Collection of Books and Illustrations of Ancient and Modern Times
work: The Kangxi Emperor and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories

front: Event: 1673 uprising led by Wu Sangui, Geng Jingzhong, and Shang Zhixin, lords who refused to surrender their vast enfeoffed lands to Kangxi
work: The Kangxi Emperor and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories

front: Ruler: revitalized the baojia mutual-surveillance system, grouping households into units of ten and a hundred, to suppress the local population
work: The Kangxi Emperor and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories

front: Treaty: 1689 pact with Russia, the Qing dynasty's first treaty with a European power, fixing much of the empire's northern border
work: The Kangxi Emperor and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories

front: Treaty: 1727 pact that reaffirmed and updated the Treaty of Nerchinsk, under which the Qing agreed to tolerate the Orthodox Church
work: The Kangxi Emperor and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories

front: Event: Oirat state led by Galdan whose forces fled the Battle of Ulan Butung after forming a "camel wall" of 10,000 tied-together camels; it declined after war with Kangxi
work: The Kangxi Emperor and the Revolt of the Three Feudatories

front: Ruler: grandson of Kangxi who restricted membership in the Eight Banner System to those of Manchu descent
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Event: series of military campaigns under Qianlong that included the extermination of the Dzungars (called "the 18th-century genocide par excellence" by scholar Mark Levene) as part of a "Pacification of Xinjiang"
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Ruler: the Ten Great Campaigns also included less successful invasions of Burma and Vietnam under his reign
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Ruler: organized Xinjiang as a province in 1884
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Ruler: employed Michel Benoist and the Jesuit Giuseppe Castiglione to help build a summer palace
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Event: 1793 British diplomatic mission that was hosted by Qianlong's corrupt favorite Heshen but was largely rejected
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Figure: Qianlong's corrupt favorite who acquired up to 1.1 million taels of silver as a director of the Siku Quanshu and was forced to commit suicide after Qianlong's abdication
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Ruler: abdicated in 1796 to avoid surpassing the reign length of his grandfather Kangxi, remaining in de facto power until 1799
work: The Qianlong Emperor and the Ten Great Campaigns

front: Event: controversy over whether Confucian ancestor veneration was compatible with Christianity, sparked by Jesuit missionaries like Matteo Ricci and ended by decrees of Pope Clement XI
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Figure: Protestant missionary who founded the China Inland Mission
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Figure: American trader imprisoned three years despite securing the execution of agents who had extorted him
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Institution: American firm that competed with the British Jardine, Matheson & Co., which nearly monopolized the opium trade
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Figure: Revolutionary War veteran who served as the first US consul, dispatched to Guangzhou in 1784
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Treaty: 1844 pact negotiated by Caleb Cushing, the first unequal treaty the US imposed on the Qing, fixing tariffs and establishing extraterritoriality
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Event: US policy formulated by John Hay urging Western powers to maintain equal trading access to China
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Event: after defeats in the Opium Wars, the Qing dynasty was forced to sign a cascade of these pacts, including those of Nanjing and Tianjin
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Event: organizations that laborers who emigrated during the Qing, including to the California gold rush, formed for mutual aid; in Southeast Asia they sometimes coalesced into self-governing states like the Lanfang Republic
work: Foreign Trade, Missionaries, and the Unequal Treaties

front: Figure: self-proclaimed brother of Jesus Christ who led the Taiping Rebellion, granting generals titles like the "Western King of 8000 Years"
work: The Taiping Rebellion

front: Figure: pushed for military modernization in response to the Taiping Rebellion
work: The Taiping Rebellion

front: Figure: reorganized the Xiang Army to fight the Taiping Rebellion
work: The Taiping Rebellion

front: Ruler: regained power as regent when a coup ended the Guangxu Emperor's Hundred Days of Reform, and diverted naval funds to rebuild the Summer Palace
work: Cixi, the Self-Strengthening Movement, and the Boxer Rebellion

front: Figure: sponsored the modernized Beiyang Fleet and was wounded by a Japanese extremist while negotiating the Treaty of Shimonoseki
work: Cixi, the Self-Strengthening Movement, and the Boxer Rebellion

front: Event: an alliance of eight nations attacked the Qing dynasty to suppress this uprising
work: Cixi, the Self-Strengthening Movement, and the Boxer Rebellion

front: Institution: secret organization considered the "brains" behind the Xinhai Revolution, which began with the Wuchang Uprising
work: The Xinhai Revolution and the Fall of the Dynasty

front: Ruler: the last Qing emperor, who abdicated after the Xinhai Revolution's success, establishing the Republic of China
work: The Xinhai Revolution and the Fall of the Dynasty

front: Figure: anti-Manchu feminist revolutionary executed for plotting the Anqing Uprising after publicly opposing foot-binding
work: The Xinhai Revolution and the Fall of the Dynasty

front: Institution: Jingdezhen porcelain manufacture restarted under this emperor in the late 17th century, producing the short-lived copper-pigmented "peach bloom" glaze
work: Art, Porcelain, and Literature

front: Work: reticulated Qing-dynasty porcelain vase decorated with panels of cavorting fish, produced under the ceramist Tang Ying's supervision
work: Art, Porcelain, and Literature

front: Concept: the orthodox "Four Wangs" were among this group of Qing painting masters, whose style contrasted with "Individualists" like Shitao
work: Art, Porcelain, and Literature

front: Artist: Jesuit who worked as a Qing court painter; later artists of the dynasty often illustrated the "Twelve Beauties of Jinling" from Dream of the Red Chamber
work: Art, Porcelain, and Literature

front: Work: jadeite carving of a cabbage with a locust and katydid, first displayed during the Qing dynasty alongside the Meat-Shaped Stone
work: Art, Porcelain, and Literature

front: Concept: genre of Qing novels satirizing scholars, such as those of the "talent and beauty" genre
work: Art, Porcelain, and Literature
